title: Directional & Resultant て-Form — てくる / ていく / てある

meta.focus: Use てくる for actions/changes directed toward the speaker or arriving in the present; ていく for actions/changes directed away or continuing into the future; てある for states created by intentional past action.

sections.title: てくる / ていく / てある — Direction and Result
sections.body: Japanese adds directional and resultant meaning to verbs by attaching くる, いく, or ある to their て-form. These three patterns express where an action is heading — toward the speaker, away from the speaker, or the lasting result left behind.

parts.text: てくる has four uses: (1) Movement toward the speaker — going somewhere, doing something, and coming back; (2) An action or change that began in the past and has reached the present; (3) The start or onset of a change; (4) Continuing an action or state up to now.

parts.en: It's gotten cold. (A change has arrived at the present.)
It started raining. (The rain has arrived / onset of change.)

parts.text: ていく has three uses: (1) Movement away from the speaker — taking something and going; (2) A change or action that will continue from now into the future; (3) An action that begins here and moves away from the present.

parts.jp: 食べ物を 持っていく。
parts.en: I'll take the food (away with me).

parts.jp: もっと 寒く なっていく。
parts.en: It will keep getting colder. (A change continues away into the future.)

parts.jp: すこしずつ おぼえていく。
parts.en: I'll keep learning little by little. (Continuing away from now.)

sections.title: てくる vs ていく — Direction

items.label: てくる
items.jp: 友だちが ケーキを 持ってきた。
items.en: My friend brought a cake here.
items.note: Action moves toward the speaker's location.

items.label: ていく
items.jp: 友だちに ケーキを 持っていく。
items.en: I'll take a cake to my friend.
items.note: Action moves away from the speaker's location.

parts.text: てある expresses a state that exists because someone intentionally performed an action. It requires a transitive verb. Crucially, the affected object takes が (not を): something が V-てある means 'something has been [done] and the result remains.' The action was purposeful — someone prepared it.

parts.jp: まどが あけてある。
parts.en: The window has been opened (intentionally, and is still open).

parts.jp: テーブルに りょうりが おいてある。
parts.en: Food has been placed on the table (and is still there).

sections.title: ている vs てある — Natural State vs Intentional State

items.label: ている
items.jp: まどが あいている。
items.en: The window is open. (Describes the current state; no agent implied.)
items.note: Uses intransitive あく. No intentional agent — it just is open.

items.label: てある
items.jp: まどが あけてある。
items.en: The window has been opened (by someone, for a reason).
items.note: Uses transitive あける. Implies intentional action — someone opened it on purpose.

examples.en: It's gotten cold. (A change has arrived at the present.)

breakdown.gloss: past of くる — arriving at now
past of くる — movement back toward speaker

examples.jp: もっと 寒く なっていく。
examples.en: It's going to keep getting colder.

breakdown.gloss: plain form of いく — continuing away into the future

examples.jp: 食べ物を 持って いく。

breakdown.gloss: plain form of いく — taking away from here

examples.jp: お茶が 入れてある。
examples.en: The tea has been prepared (and is ready).

breakdown.gloss: て-form of 入れる (to put in/pour)
ある — resultant state from intentional action

sections.title: Weather Changes and the Mountain Trip (Casual)

lines.jp: 寒く なってきたね。もう 秋だよ。
lines.en: It's gotten cold, hasn't it. Autumn has arrived already!

lines.jp: そうだね。寒く なっていくね。
lines.en: Yeah. It's just going to keep getting colder.

lines.jp: 来週、山に 旅行に 行くんだ。食べ物を 持って 行くよ。
lines.en: Next week I'm going on a trip to the mountains. I'll take food along.

lines.jp: いいな！お茶も 持って 行く？
lines.en: Nice! Are you taking tea along too?

lines.jp: うん。お茶は もう 入れて あるよ。
lines.en: Yeah. The tea has already been prepared and is ready.

sections.instructions: Choose the correct てくる / ていく / てある form to complete each sentence.

items.explanation: てくる (てきた = past) expresses a change that has arrived at the present: なってきた。
ていく expresses movement or action directed away from the speaker: 持っていく (carry and go)。

items.before: お茶は もう 入れ
items.translation: The tea has already been prepared.
items.explanation: てある expresses a state resulting from intentional action: 入れてある (was poured/prepared, and the result remains)。

sections.instructions: Choose the best answer for each question.

items.explanation: てくる requires the て-form of the preceding verb: なって (て-form of なる) + きた。

items.q: お茶 ____ もう 入れてある。
items.explanation: The affected object in a てある sentence takes が, not を: お茶が もう 入れてある。

items.q: お茶が 入れてある。— What does this sentence express?
choices: The tea has been prepared (result of intentional action)
Someone is making tea right now
Tea was made and brought here
items.answer: The tea has been prepared (result of intentional action)
items.explanation: てある expresses a resultant state from intentional action — the tea was put in/prepared and the effect remains.

items.q: 食べ物を 持って 行く。— What does this mean?
choices: I will take food (along with me)
items.answer: I will take food (along with me)
items.explanation: 持っていく means to carry something away from the speaker's location: 持って (て-form of 持つ) + 行く (to go)。
